title: Tenant Rights in Ireland — Residential Tenancies Board
content:
Tenants in Ireland have a range of legal rights protected under the Residential Tenancies Acts 2004-2022. These rights apply to most private rental tenancies and some approved housing body tenancies.

Right to a rent book
Your landlord must provide a rent book at the start of the tenancy. The rent book records rent payments and confirms key tenancy details. If your landlord does not provide one, you can request it — failure to provide a rent book is an offence.

Right to quiet enjoyment
You have the right to peaceful and exclusive enjoyment of your rented home. Your landlord cannot enter the property without your permission or without giving you 24 hours' advance notice (except in genuine emergencies).

Right to know who your landlord is
You are entitled to know the full name and address of your landlord. If a letting agent manages the property, you are also entitled to the landlord's personal contact details.

Protection from illegal eviction
A landlord cannot remove you from the property without following the proper legal process. They must issue a valid written Notice of Termination. Changing the locks, removing your belongings, or cutting off utilities to force you out is illegal — this is called illegal eviction and is a criminal offence.

Security of tenure
Once you have been renting for 6 months, you gain security of tenure. This means your landlord can only terminate the tenancy on specific grounds, including:
- You have not paid rent
- You have breached your obligations under the tenancy
- The landlord needs the property for their own or immediate family use
- The landlord intends to sell the property
- The landlord intends to refurbish the property and it requires vacant possession
- The property is no longer suitable for your needs

Cycle of security (Part 4 tenancy)
After 6 months, you have a right to remain for up to 6 years (a 'Part 4 tenancy'). After 6 years, you enter a new 6-year cycle with the same protections.

Standards for rental accommodation
Landlords must maintain the property in good repair and ensure it meets minimum standards, including adequate heating, sanitation, cooking facilities, and ventilation. The property must have a valid BER (Building Energy Rating) certificate, which must be shown to prospective tenants.

Deposit protection
Your landlord can hold a deposit of up to one month's rent. The deposit can only be retained to cover rent arrears, damage beyond normal wear and tear, or utility bills left unpaid by the tenant. The deposit must be returned promptly at the end of the tenancy if there are no valid deductions.

title: Rent Pressure Zones (RPZs) — Residential Tenancies Board
content:
Rent Pressure Zones (RPZs) are designated areas of Ireland where rent increases are restricted. RPZs were introduced to slow rent increases in areas of high housing demand.

What areas are RPZs
As of 2025, the vast majority of Ireland — including all four Dublin local authority areas, Cork City, and many other urban areas — are designated Rent Pressure Zones. The full list of RPZ areas is published by the RTB and updated by the Minister for Housing.

Rent increase limit in an RPZ
In a Rent Pressure Zone, a landlord may not increase rent by more than the general inflation rate (as measured by the Harmonised Index of Consumer Prices, HICP) or 2% per year — whichever is lower.

This effectively caps annual rent increases at approximately 2% in most cases.

Minimum period between rent reviews
A landlord cannot review the rent more than once every 24 months. A new tenancy starts a fresh 24-month period.

New tenancies in an RPZ
When a new tenancy starts on a property in an RPZ, the landlord can only charge rent up to the amount the previous tenant paid (plus any legally permitted increases since that tenancy began). The landlord must provide a rent setting notice to the new tenant explaining how the rent was calculated.

Exemptions from RPZ restrictions
The RPZ restrictions do not apply if:
- The property was substantially refurbished (the landlord must show evidence and register this with the RTB)
- The property has not previously been let as a residential dwelling
- The property was converted from a non-residential use

Complaining about an unlawful rent increase
If you believe your landlord has increased your rent unlawfully in an RPZ, you can dispute it through the RTB. Disputes must be raised within 28 days of the rent increase taking effect.

RPZ and properties outside RPZs
For properties in non-RPZ areas, there is no legal cap on rent increases, but landlords must still give proper notice and cannot increase the rent more than once every 24 months.

Checking if a property is in an RPZ
Use the RTB's RPZ Checker tool on rtb.ie to check whether a specific address is in a Rent Pressure Zone.

title: Notice Periods for Ending a Tenancy — Residential Tenancies Board
content:
A tenancy can only be ended by a landlord or tenant following the legal notice requirements under the Residential Tenancies Acts. Both landlords and tenants must give written notice.

Tenant notice periods
If you (the tenant) want to end your tenancy, you must give the following minimum notice:
- Tenancy duration under 6 months: 28 days' notice
- Tenancy duration 6 months to 1 year: 35 days' notice
- Tenancy duration 1 to 2 years: 42 days' notice
- Tenancy duration 2 to 3 years: 56 days' notice
- Tenancy duration 3 to 4 years: 84 days' notice
- Tenancy duration 4 to 5 years: 112 days' notice
- Tenancy duration 5 to 6 years: 140 days' notice
- Tenancy duration over 6 years: 196 days' notice

Landlord notice periods
If a landlord wants to terminate a tenancy after the tenant has been in occupation for 6 months, the landlord must:
1. Have a valid ground for termination (see below)
2. Give longer notice than tenants are required to give

Landlord notice periods:
- Tenancy under 6 months: 28 days' notice
- 6 months to 1 year: 90 days' notice
- 1 to 3 years: 120 days' notice
- 3 to 7 years: 180 days' notice
- Over 7 years: 196 days' notice

Valid grounds for a landlord to end a tenancy
A landlord can only terminate a tenancy (after the first 6 months) on specific grounds:
- Rent arrears
- Tenant breach of obligations
- Property needed for landlord or immediate family member's own use
- Landlord intends to sell the property within 9 months
- Landlord intends to substantially refurbish the property
- Property no longer suited to tenant's accommodation needs (e.g., overcrowded or under-occupied)

Notice of Termination requirements
A valid Notice of Termination must be:
- In writing
- Signed by the landlord or their agent
- State the reason for termination (for landlords terminating after 6 months)
- Specify the date the tenancy will end
- Include the RTB's dispute resolution information

Invalid notices
If a notice does not meet the legal requirements, it is invalid and you do not have to vacate. You can dispute an invalid notice through the RTB.

Overholding
If a tenant remains in the property after the valid notice period expires without agreement, this is called overholding. A landlord can refer an overholding dispute to the RTB, which can issue an order requiring the tenant to vacate.

title: RTB Dispute Resolution — Residential Tenancies Board
content:
The Residential Tenancies Board (RTB) provides a dispute resolution service for tenants and landlords. Disputes about tenancies must generally be referred to the RTB before going to court.

Types of disputes the RTB handles
- Rent arrears and unpaid rent
- Illegal eviction
- Deposit retention (landlord refusing to return deposit)
- Overholding (tenant remaining after notice period)
- Breach of tenant obligations (damage, anti-social behaviour)
- Breach of landlord obligations (disrepair, failure to maintain standards)
- Invalid notice of termination
- Unlawful rent increases (including in Rent Pressure Zones)

Time limit for making a dispute application
You must apply to the RTB within 12 months of the dispute arising. For some dispute types, a shorter period applies — check the RTB website for specific limits.

Stage 1: Mediation
For some disputes, the RTB offers mediation as a first step. A mediator works with both parties to reach a voluntary agreement. Mediation is faster than adjudication and decisions are binding if both parties agree.

Stage 2: Adjudication
If mediation fails or is not suitable, the dispute goes to an Adjudicator. The Adjudicator is an independent expert appointed by the RTB. Both parties can submit evidence and make oral submissions. The Adjudicator issues a written determination order. This is free for the parties.

Stage 3: Tribunal
Either party can appeal an Adjudicator's decision to a Tribunal (a panel of three people). A Tribunal appeal must be made within 21 days of receiving the Adjudication determination. The Tribunal conducts a full hearing. Tribunal determinations can be appealed to the High Court on a point of law only.

Enforcement
If a party does not comply with an RTB determination order, the other party can apply to the Circuit Court to enforce it. The RTB can also refer cases to the Gardaí in serious cases of illegal eviction.

How to apply
Apply online through the RTB's dispute resolution portal at rtb.ie. You will need your tenancy registration number (if the tenancy is registered), details of the dispute, and supporting evidence (photographs, correspondence, receipts).

Fees
Dispute resolution through the RTB is free. There is no charge for adjudication or mediation.

title: Tenancy Deposits — What You Are Entitled To — Residential Tenancies Board
content:
A deposit is a sum of money paid by a tenant to a landlord before a tenancy begins. Under Irish law, deposits are regulated and landlords can only retain them in specific circumstances.

Maximum deposit
The maximum deposit a landlord can charge is equivalent to one month's rent. This applies to all private rental tenancies. Landlords cannot request additional deposits or top-up payments above this amount.

When a landlord can retain your deposit
A landlord may deduct from your deposit only to cover:
1. Rent arrears — if you owe unpaid rent at the end of the tenancy
2. Damage beyond normal wear and tear — for example, broken furniture, stains, or holes in walls that go beyond ordinary use
3. Outstanding utility bills — if you left energy or other bills unpaid

Normal wear and tear
A landlord cannot deduct from your deposit for normal wear and tear — this refers to the gradual deterioration that happens through ordinary use of the property. Examples of normal wear and tear include:
- Minor scuffs on walls
- Faded carpets after years of use
- Worn upholstery
- Small nail holes from pictures

Examples of damage beyond normal wear and tear:
- Broken fixtures, appliances, or furniture
- Large stains on carpets or walls
- Burns or cigarette damage
- Pet damage

Timeline for returning deposits
Your landlord must return your deposit promptly after the tenancy ends. Irish law does not set a specific number of days, but the RTB expects deposits to be returned within a reasonable time — in practice, within 2-4 weeks of vacating and returning the keys.

Protecting yourself
When you move in, document the condition of the property with dated photographs before signing any agreements. Keep a copy of your lease and inventory. This evidence is essential if you need to dispute deposit retention.

Disputing deposit retention
If your landlord refuses to return your deposit without a valid reason:
1. Write to your landlord requesting the deposit back, explaining you believe there is no valid reason for retention
2. If they still refuse, apply to the RTB's dispute resolution service within 12 months of the dispute arising
3. The RTB can order the landlord to return the deposit

The RTB dispute resolution service is free.
